title: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of True Neurogenic Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS)
items.content: True neurogenic TOS is caused by compression of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus or C8/T1 nerve roots, typically due to an anomalous cartilaginous band. Diagnosis involves identifying characteristic physical signs and confirming with imaging and electrophysiological studies.
keyPoints: Compression often results from a cartilaginous band connecting an elongate transverse process at C7 with the first rib.
Pain is mild or absent; key signs include weakness and wasting of intrinsic hand muscles, and diminished sensation on the palmar aspect of the fifth digit.
Diagnosis confirmed by anteroposterior cervical spine x-ray showing an elongate C7 transverse process, and EMG/NCSs.
Treatment involves surgical resection of the anomalous band to halt progression of weakness.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · THORACIC OUTLET SYNDROMES

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Arterial Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS)
items.content: Arterial TOS is caused by subclavian artery compression, often due to a cervical rib, leading to poststenotic dilatation and possible thrombus formation. Diagnosis involves imaging studies and management includes medical and surgical interventions.
keyPoints: Compression of the subclavian artery typically results from a cervical rib.
Symptoms include reduced blood pressure in the affected limb and signs of emboli in the hand; neurologic signs are absent.
Diagnosis confirmed by ultrasound, which can noninvasively show arterial changes.
Treatment includes thrombolysis or anticoagulation (with or without embolectomy) and surgical excision of the compressing cervical rib.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · THORACIC OUTLET SYNDROMES

items.title: Neoplastic Infiltration of the Brachial Plexus: Recognizing Pain and Weakness Patterns
items.content: Injury to the lower trunk of the brachial plexus due to neoplastic infiltration can manifest as shoulder or supraclavicular pain radiating down the arm, numbness in the fourth and fifth fingers or medial forearm, and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles. This pattern is critical for distinguishing from cervical radiculopathy.
keyPoints: Pain begins distal to the posterior neck region in the shoulder girdle or upper arm.
Numbness involves the fourth and fifth fingers or medial forearm.
Weakness affects intrinsic hand muscles innervated by the lower trunk and medial cord of the brachial plexus.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · BRACHIAL PLEXUS AND NERVES

items.subtopic: BRACHIAL PLEXUS AND ACUTE BRACHIAL NEURITIS
items.title: Acute Brachial Neuritis: Differentiating from Radiculopathy
items.content: Acute brachial neuritis presents with severe shoulder or scapular pain followed by weakness in the proximal arm and shoulder girdle muscles. It can be preceded by infection, vaccination, or minor surgery, and may lead to a winged scapula if the long thoracic nerve is affected.
keyPoints: Acute onset of severe shoulder or scapular pain.
Weakness in proximal arm and shoulder girdle muscles innervated by the upper brachial plexus.
Possible preceding events: infection, vaccination, minor surgical procedure.
Long thoracic nerve involvement can result in a winged scapula.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · BRACHIAL PLEXUS AND NERVES

items.title: Differentiating Shoulder Pain from Spinal Pain
items.content: When shoulder pain mimics spinal pain but lacks symptoms of radiculopathy, consider mechanical shoulder conditions or referred pain. Mechanical shoulder pain includes bicipital tendonitis, frozen shoulder, bursitis, rotator cuff tear, dislocation, adhesive capsulitis, and impingement. Referred pain sources include subdiaphragmatic irritation, angina, or Pancoast tumor.
keyPoints: Absence of radiculopathy symptoms suggests mechanical shoulder conditions or referred pain.
Mechanical shoulder pain often worsens at night and with specific movements (passive abduction, internal rotation, extension).
Normal passive full range of motion without worsening pain helps exclude mechanical pathology as a cause of neck region pain.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · SHOULDER

items.title: Identifying Mechanical Shoulder Pain
items.content: Mechanical shoulder pain is characterized by night-time exacerbation, local tenderness, and aggravation with specific arm movements. Conditions include bicipital tendonitis, frozen shoulder, bursitis, rotator cuff tear, dislocation, adhesive capsulitis, or impingement.
keyPoints: Mechanical shoulder pain often worsens at night.
Associated with local shoulder tenderness and aggravated by passive abduction, internal rotation, or extension of the arm.
Normal passive full range of motion without worsening pain can exclude mechanical shoulder pathology.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · SHOULDER

items.question: Which imaging study can show an elongate C7 transverse process as an anatomical marker for neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome?
items.explanation: An anteroposterior cervical spine x-ray will show an elongate C7 transverse process, which is a marker for neurogenic TOS.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · THORACIC OUTLET SYNDROMES

items.question: What is the primary treatment for arterial thoracic outlet syndrome?
options: Surgical resection of the anomalous band
Thrombolysis or anticoagulation and surgical excision of the cervical rib
items.explanation: Treatment for arterial TOS includes thrombolysis or anticoagulation (with or without embolectomy) and surgical excision of the compressing cervical rib.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · THORACIC OUTLET SYNDROMES

items.question: Which diagnostic test is considered the choice for venous thoracic outlet syndrome?
items.explanation: Venography is the diagnostic test of choice for venous TOS.
items.reference: PAIN ARISING IN THE SHOULDER REGION · THORACIC OUTLET SYNDROMES

items.question: Which condition is associated with shoulder or supraclavicular pain radiating down the arm, numbness of the fourth and fifth fingers, and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles?
options: Neoplastic infiltration of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus
Acute brachial neuritis preceded by vaccination
Carpal tunnel syndrome mimicking C5 or C6 root lesion
items.explanation: Neoplastic infiltration of the lower trunk can produce these symptoms.

items.question: Which syndrome is often confused with radiculopathy due to its acute onset and progression?
items.explanation: Acute brachial neuritis is often confused with radiculopathy.

items.question: Which condition can produce a winged scapula due to nerve involvement?
options: Neoplastic infiltration of the lower trunk
Acute brachial neuritis affecting the long thoracic nerve
items.explanation: Acute brachial neuritis can affect the long thoracic nerve, causing a winged scapula.

items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with mechanical shoulder pain?
items.explanation: Radiculopathy is not a cause of mechanical shoulder pain; it involves nerve root issues.

items.question: Which symptom helps exclude mechanical shoulder pathology as the cause of neck region pain?
options: Worsening pain with passive full range of motion
Normal passive full range of motion without worsening pain
items.explanation: Demonstrating normal passive full range of motion of the arm at the shoulder without worsening of the pain can help exclude mechanical shoulder pathology.

items.question: Which condition is a cause of referred pain from the shoulder?
items.explanation: Subdiaphragmatic irritation is a cause of referred pain from the shoulder.
